title: Variables
summary: Give a value a name so you can use it again.
refresher: A variable is a name that holds a value. You create one by assigning to it: `score = 10`. The name goes on the left, the value on the right, and the `=` means "put this value in this name". It is not a comparison. Assigning to a name that already exists replaces what was there. Using a name you never assigned is an error, because Python has nothing to look up.

blocks.body: A variable is a name for a value. You make one by assigning: put the name on the left, an equals sign in the middle, and the value on the right. From then on, writing the name means the value.

blocks.caption: Three variables, three types of value. Python works out the type on its own, so you never have to announce it.
blocks.code:
name = "Ada"
age = 36
is_student = True

print(name)
print(age)

blocks.title: The equals sign does not mean equals
blocks.body: In math, `x = 5` states a fact. In Python it gives an order: put 5 into x. That difference is why `x = x + 1` is not nonsense. It reads as "take what x is now, add one, and put the result back into x". Later, when you learn `==`, this is the distinction you will be leaning on: `=` puts a value in, `==` asks whether two values match.

A variable can be reassigned as often as you like. The new value replaces the old one, and the old value is simply gone. Nothing warns you, because replacing a value is usually exactly what you meant to do.

blocks.caption: Each line runs top to bottom, so the value you get depends on when you look. Reading `count` before the last line would have given you 1, not 2.

blocks.code:
total = 5
total = total + 3
total = total * 2
print(total)
blocks.why: Work through it one line at a time, which is exactly how Python does. `total` starts at 5. Then it becomes 5 + 3, which is 8. Then it becomes 8 * 2, which is 16. The trick is that each line uses the value from the line before, not the original 5.

blocks.body: Names are not arbitrary. Python has rules about which ones are allowed, and there is a strong convention about which ones you should actually pick.

blocks.title: Naming rules and habits
items: Letters, digits, and underscores only. No spaces and no dashes.
It cannot start with a digit. `score2` is fine, `2score` is not.
Case matters. `score` and `Score` are two different variables.
Python style is lowercase with underscores: `final_score`, not `finalScore`.
Say what it holds. `n` tells the next reader nothing; `student_count` tells them everything.

blocks.title: Using a name you never assigned
blocks.body: If you type a name Python has never seen, you get a NameError. Nine times out of ten it is a typo: you assigned `total` and then asked for `totl`. The error message names the variable it could not find, so read it and compare it letter by letter against where you defined it. The other cause is asking for a variable before the line that creates it has run.

blocks.caption: Python reads top to bottom. A variable does not exist until the assignment line has actually run, so asking for it before then is asking for something that is not there yet.
blocks.wrong:
print(greeting)
greeting = "Hi"
# asked for it one
# line too early
blocks.right:
greeting = "Hi"
print(greeting)
# created first,
# then used

blocks.prompt: Which of these lines is a syntax error?
choices: None of them, all four are legal
blocks.why: A name cannot start with a digit, so `1st_place` is rejected before the program even runs. `firstPlace` and `first_place2` are both legal. `firstPlace` is against Python's style convention, which prefers underscores, but style is advice and syntax is a rule. Python only refuses to run the one that breaks the rule.

blocks.body: One last habit worth starting now. When a value in your program has a meaning, give it a name even if you only use it once. `price * 0.06` makes a reader guess. `price * sales_tax_rate` does not.
